behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is HTTP handler + signature verifier + queue handoff. MOCK-MISS: common real bug is verifying parsed JSON instead of raw body, which appears at module boundaries. REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: no real OS/network/subprocess required, no AI output, no agent pipeline. Rejected System because behavior text is only request validation/forwarding, not real network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler in-process with real middleware chain that exposes raw body bytes; send a signed request where body is mutated after signing. Assert HTTP 401 and assert enqueue method was not called.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: if verification is a single pure function, Unit could suffice. Top2 contrast: Integration beats Unit because the failure is often raw-body handoff between middleware and verifier; flip to Unit if behavior explicitly said only `verifySignature(rawBody, header)` logic is under test.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is ordering across handler and queue modules; test must observe enqueue completion before response send. REAL-INFRA: real OS/network not required to catch await/order bugs. LLM/MULTI-STEP: not applicable. Rejected System because behavior text does not require external queue transport semantics, only pre-ack write ordering.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process HTTP app with a controllable queue adapter whose `enqueue` promise resolves on command. Verify response is not completed before `enqueue` resolves, and completes after resolution.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: if persistent durability guarantees are required (fsync/remote ACK), System may be needed. Top2 contrast: Integration wins because stated behavior is sequencing, not storage-engine durability; flip to System if text required surviving process crash immediately after 200.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is decoupling between ingress handler and downstream processor completion. MOCK-MISS: bug appears when handler accidentally awaits processor promise. No real infrastructure or AI dependency required. Rejected System because behavior text is about async control flow, not real network/OS primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + worker trigger in-process. Make downstream processing block on a deferred promise. Assert HTTP response returns before deferred resolves, while processing eventually completes later.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: could be Unit if handler alone controls await/not-await. Top2 contrast: Integration wins because contract spans handler and processing wiring; flip to Unit if downstream call is fully mocked and only one function’s await behavior is under test.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA dominates: requirement is wall-clock latency under concurrency, which depends on runtime scheduling/event-loop behavior and can be hidden by fakes. MOCK-HIDE: mocked timing/concurrency can pass while real server misses SLA. No AI dependency. Runner-up Integration rejected because behavior explicitly requires real-time bound under concurrent slow workload.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process, generate concurrent prior events that force slow downstream work, then send a new valid webhook over real HTTP and measure end-to-end latency; assert response <3s.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: if deterministic fake clock and single-process scheduler fully model production, Integration could suffice. Top2 contrast: System wins because wall-clock + concurrency SLO is explicitly in behavior; flip to Integration if requirement changed to logical timeout flag rather than measured elapsed time.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness sits at handler + idempotency store/queue boundary. Unit can miss state-handoff bugs (lookup/write order). Integration-brake: no required OS/network/subprocess, no AI output, no multi-agent sequence. Rejected System because text does not require distributed race or cross-process behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Use real handler and idempotency persistence module in-process; submit same event ID twice and assert only one queue write record exists and second call is treated as duplicate.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: concurrent duplicates across instances might require System. Top2 contrast: Integration wins for single-service idempotency contract in text; flip to System if behavior added 'across multiple service instances under concurrent delivery'.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is translation of queue-full signal into HTTP status/header across module boundary. Mock-miss exists at mapping layer, not pure function only. Integration-brake checks: no real OS/network/subprocess required; no AI; no agentic chain. Rejected System because text does not require proving real queue saturation, only handling reported-full condition.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module that can emit canonical 'full' error. Send request and assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of `Retry-After`; assert event not enqueued.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: if queue full signal varies only in real backend protocol, System may be needed. Top2 contrast: Integration wins because behavior begins with 'when the queue reports'; flip to System if behavior required inducing actual saturation instead of simulated report.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Target is queue implementation behavior against real persistence layer; minimum is modules + real local storage semantics. Integration includes local DB/filesystem and catches config/constraint enforcement bugs. Rejected System because behavior text does not require external networked queue or subprocess-managed service.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real queue implementation against its real local persistence backend, set small capacity, enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert explicit rejection and no over-capacity state.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Adversarial critique: if queue backend is external service (Redis/SQS), System would be minimum. Top2 contrast: Integration wins when backend is embeddable/local persistence; flip to System if configured queue is remote and capacity enforcement happens only server-side.
